original_abstract: Some may argue that they might be complementary in that decolonial approaches could ideally create more space for political debates within which decentring approaches could then advance pragmatic improvements and induce gradual evolutions towards systemic change. There is however no guarantee that this would happen, especially if decentring and post/decolonial agendas continue to be conflated. While decentring may lead to a more legitimate and effective EU foreign policy, it may also not diminish or may even reinforce the coloniality of EU power. Thus, rather than ‘decentring Europe’, we ought to move towards decolonial futures that truly prioritize the democratic struggle against colonial continuities.
humanized_abstract: Some might think that decolonial approaches could be complementary to political discussions around the EU, in the sense that decentring approaches would then be able to lead to concrete improvements and achieve a gradual transformation of the system. But there is no guarantee that this will be the case, as long as decentring and post/decolonial agendas are confused. In fact, an effective and legitimate EU foreign policy does not necessarily mean that the coloniality of EU power will be diminished. Rather than “decentring Europe”, we need to aim at decolonial futures that serve the democratic struggle against colonial continuities.

But there is no guarantee that this will be the case, as long as decentring and post/decolonial agendas are confused. In fact, an effective and legitimate EU foreign policy does not necessarily mean that the coloniality of EU power will be diminished. Rather than “decentring Europe”, we need to aim at decolonial futures that serve the democratic struggle against colonial continuities.
